src/backend/rewrite/rewriteHandler.c
1/*-------------------------------------------------------------------------
2 *
3 * rewriteHandler.c
4 *		Primary module of query rewriter.
5 *
6 * Portions Copyright (c) 1996-2025, PostgreSQL Global Development Group
7 * Portions Copyright (c) 1994, Regents of the University of California
8 *
9 * IDENTIFICATION
10 *	  src/backend/rewrite/rewriteHandler.c
11 *
12 * NOTES
13 *	  Some of the terms used in this file are of historic nature: "retrieve"
14 *	  was the PostQUEL keyword for what today is SELECT. "RIR" stands for
15 *	  "Retrieve-Instead-Retrieve", that is an ON SELECT DO INSTEAD SELECT rule
16 *	  (which has to be unconditional and where only one rule can exist on each
17 *	  relation).
18 *
19 *-------------------------------------------------------------------------
20 */
21#include "postgres.h"
22
23#include "access/relation.h"
24#include "access/sysattr.h"
25#include "access/table.h"
26#include "catalog/dependency.h"
27#include "commands/trigger.h"
28#include "executor/executor.h"
29#include "foreign/fdwapi.h"
30#include "miscadmin.h"
31#include "nodes/makefuncs.h"
32#include "nodes/nodeFuncs.h"
33#include "optimizer/optimizer.h"
34#include "parser/analyze.h"
35#include "parser/parse_coerce.h"
36#include "parser/parse_relation.h"
37#include "parser/parsetree.h"
38#include "rewrite/rewriteDefine.h"
39#include "rewrite/rewriteHandler.h"
40#include "rewrite/rewriteManip.h"
41#include "rewrite/rewriteSearchCycle.h"
42#include "rewrite/rowsecurity.h"
43#include "tcop/tcopprot.h"
44#include "utils/builtins.h"
45#include "utils/lsyscache.h"
46#include "utils/rel.h"
47
48
49/* We use a list of these to detect recursion in RewriteQuery */
50typedef struct rewrite_event
51{
52	Oid			relation;		/* OID of relation having rules */
53	CmdType		event;			/* type of rule being fired */
54} rewrite_event;
55
56typedef struct acquireLocksOnSubLinks_context
57{
58	bool		for_execute;	/* AcquireRewriteLocks' forExecute param */
59} acquireLocksOnSubLinks_context;
60
61typedef struct fireRIRonSubLink_context
62{
63	List	   *activeRIRs;
64	bool		hasRowSecurity;
65} fireRIRonSubLink_context;
66
67static bool acquireLocksOnSubLinks(Node *node,
68								   acquireLocksOnSubLinks_context *context);
69static Query *rewriteRuleAction(Query *parsetree,
70								Query *rule_action,
71								Node *rule_qual,
72								int rt_index,
73								CmdType event,
74								bool *returning_flag);
75static List *adjustJoinTreeList(Query *parsetree, bool removert, int rt_index);
76static List *rewriteTargetListIU(List *targetList,
77								 CmdType commandType,
78								 OverridingKind override,
79								 Relation target_relation,
80								 RangeTblEntry *values_rte,
81								 int values_rte_index,
82								 Bitmapset **unused_values_attrnos);
83static TargetEntry *process_matched_tle(TargetEntry *src_tle,
84										TargetEntry *prior_tle,
85										const char *attrName);
86static Node *get_assignment_input(Node *node);
87static Bitmapset *findDefaultOnlyColumns(RangeTblEntry *rte);
88static bool rewriteValuesRTE(Query *parsetree, RangeTblEntry *rte, int rti,
89							 Relation target_relation,
90							 Bitmapset *unused_cols);
91static void rewriteValuesRTEToNulls(Query *parsetree, RangeTblEntry *rte);
92static void markQueryForLocking(Query *qry, Node *jtnode,
93								LockClauseStrength strength, LockWaitPolicy waitPolicy,
94								bool pushedDown);
95static List *matchLocks(CmdType event, Relation relation,
96						int varno, Query *parsetree, bool *hasUpdate);
97static Query *fireRIRrules(Query *parsetree, List *activeRIRs);
98static Bitmapset *adjust_view_column_set(Bitmapset *cols, List *targetlist);
99static List *get_generated_columns(Relation rel, int rt_index, bool include_stored);
100
101
102/*
103 * AcquireRewriteLocks -
104 *	  Acquire suitable locks on all the relations mentioned in the Query.
105 *	  These locks will ensure that the relation schemas don't change under us
106 *	  while we are rewriting, planning, and executing the query.
107 *
108 * Caution: this may modify the querytree, therefore caller should usually
109 * have done a copyObject() to make a writable copy of the querytree in the
110 * current memory context.
111 *
112 * forExecute indicates that the query is about to be executed.  If so,
113 * we'll acquire the lock modes specified in the RTE rellockmode fields.
114 * If forExecute is false, AccessShareLock is acquired on all relations.
115 * This case is suitable for ruleutils.c, for example, where we only need
116 * schema stability and we don't intend to actually modify any relations.
117 *
118 * forUpdatePushedDown indicates that a pushed-down FOR [KEY] UPDATE/SHARE
119 * applies to the current subquery, requiring all rels to be opened with at
120 * least RowShareLock.  This should always be false at the top of the
121 * recursion.  When it is true, we adjust RTE rellockmode fields to reflect
122 * the higher lock level.  This flag is ignored if forExecute is false.
123 *
124 * A secondary purpose of this routine is to fix up JOIN RTE references to
125 * dropped columns (see details below).  Such RTEs are modified in-place.
126 *
127 * This processing can, and for efficiency's sake should, be skipped when the
128 * querytree has just been built by the parser: parse analysis already got
129 * all the same locks we'd get here, and the parser will have omitted dropped
130 * columns from JOINs to begin with.  But we must do this whenever we are
131 * dealing with a querytree produced earlier than the current command.
132 *
133 * About JOINs and dropped columns: although the parser never includes an
134 * already-dropped column in a JOIN RTE's alias var list, it is possible for
135 * such a list in a stored rule to include references to dropped columns.
136 * (If the column is not explicitly referenced anywhere else in the query,
137 * the dependency mechanism won't consider it used by the rule and so won't
138 * prevent the column drop.)  To support get_rte_attribute_is_dropped(), we
139 * replace join alias vars that reference dropped columns with null pointers.
140 *
141 * (In PostgreSQL 8.0, we did not do this processing but instead had
142 * get_rte_attribute_is_dropped() recurse to detect dropped columns in joins.
143 * That approach had horrible performance unfortunately; in particular
144 * construction of a nested join was O(N^2) in the nesting depth.)
145 */
146void
147AcquireRewriteLocks(Query *parsetree,
148					bool forExecute,
149					bool forUpdatePushedDown)
150{
151	ListCell   *l;
152	int			rt_index;
153	acquireLocksOnSubLinks_context context;
154
155	context.for_execute = forExecute;
156
157	/*
158	 * First, process RTEs of the current query level.
159	 */
160	rt_index = 0;
161	foreach(l, parsetree->rtable)
162	{
163		RangeTblEntry *rte = (RangeTblEntry *) lfirst(l);
164		Relation	rel;
165		LOCKMODE	lockmode;
166		List	   *newaliasvars;
167		Index		curinputvarno;
168		RangeTblEntry *curinputrte;
169		ListCell   *ll;
170
171		++rt_index;
172		switch (rte->rtekind)
173		{
174			case RTE_RELATION:
175
176				/*
177				 * Grab the appropriate lock type for the relation, and do not
178				 * release it until end of transaction.  This protects the
179				 * rewriter, planner, and executor against schema changes
180				 * mid-query.
181				 *
182				 * If forExecute is false, ignore rellockmode and just use
183				 * AccessShareLock.
184				 */
185				if (!forExecute)
186					lockmode = AccessShareLock;
187				else if (forUpdatePushedDown)
188				{
189					/* Upgrade RTE's lock mode to reflect pushed-down lock */
190					if (rte->rellockmode == AccessShareLock)
191						rte->rellockmode = RowShareLock;
192					lockmode = rte->rellockmode;
193				}
194				else
195					lockmode = rte->rellockmode;
196
197				rel = table_open(rte->relid, lockmode);
198
199				/*
200				 * While we have the relation open, update the RTE's relkind,
201				 * just in case it changed since this rule was made.
202				 */
203				rte->relkind = rel->rd_rel->relkind;
204
205				table_close(rel, NoLock);
206				break;
207
208			case RTE_JOIN:
209
210				/*
211				 * Scan the join's alias var list to see if any columns have
212				 * been dropped, and if so replace those Vars with null
213				 * pointers.
214				 *
215				 * Since a join has only two inputs, we can expect to see
216				 * multiple references to the same input RTE; optimize away
217				 * multiple fetches.
218				 */
219				newaliasvars = NIL;
220				curinputvarno = 0;
221				curinputrte = NULL;
222				foreach(ll, rte->joinaliasvars)
223				{
224					Var		   *aliasitem = (Var *) lfirst(ll);
225					Var		   *aliasvar = aliasitem;
226
227					/* Look through any implicit coercion */
228					aliasvar = (Var *) strip_implicit_coercions((Node *) aliasvar);
229
230					/*
231					 * If the list item isn't a simple Var, then it must
232					 * represent a merged column, ie a USING column, and so it
233					 * couldn't possibly be dropped, since it's referenced in
234					 * the join clause.  (Conceivably it could also be a null
235					 * pointer already?  But that's OK too.)
236					 */
237					if (aliasvar && IsA(aliasvar, Var))
238					{
239						/*
240						 * The elements of an alias list have to refer to
241						 * earlier RTEs of the same rtable, because that's the
242						 * order the planner builds things in.  So we already
243						 * processed the referenced RTE, and so it's safe to
244						 * use get_rte_attribute_is_dropped on it. (This might
245						 * not hold after rewriting or planning, but it's OK
246						 * to assume here.)
247						 */
248						Assert(aliasvar->varlevelsup == 0);
249						if (aliasvar->varno != curinputvarno)
250						{
251							curinputvarno = aliasvar->varno;
252							if (curinputvarno >= rt_index)
253								elog(ERROR, "unexpected varno %d in JOIN RTE %d",
254									 curinputvarno, rt_index);
255							curinputrte = rt_fetch(curinputvarno,
256												   parsetree->rtable);
257						}
258						if (get_rte_attribute_is_dropped(curinputrte,
259														 aliasvar->varattno))
260						{
261							/* Replace the join alias item with a NULL */
262							aliasitem = NULL;
263						}
264					}
265					newaliasvars = lappend(newaliasvars, aliasitem);
266				}
267				rte->joinaliasvars = newaliasvars;
268				break;
269
270			case RTE_SUBQUERY:
271
272				/*
273				 * The subquery RTE itself is all right, but we have to
274				 * recurse to process the represented subquery.
275				 */
276				AcquireRewriteLocks(rte->subquery,
277									forExecute,
278									(forUpdatePushedDown ||
279									 get_parse_rowmark(parsetree, rt_index) != NULL));
280				break;
281
282			default:
283				/* ignore other types of RTEs */
284				break;
285		}
286	}
287
288	/* Recurse into subqueries in WITH */
289	foreach(l, parsetree->cteList)
290	{
291		CommonTableExpr *cte = (CommonTableExpr *) lfirst(l);
292
293		AcquireRewriteLocks((Query *) cte->ctequery, forExecute, false);
294	}
295
296	/*
297	 * Recurse into sublink subqueries, too.  But we already did the ones in
298	 * the rtable and cteList.
299	 */
300	if (parsetree->hasSubLinks)
301		query_tree_walker(parsetree, acquireLocksOnSubLinks, &context,
302						  QTW_IGNORE_RC_SUBQUERIES);
303}
304
305/*
306 * Walker to find sublink subqueries for AcquireRewriteLocks
307 */
308static bool
309acquireLocksOnSubLinks(Node *node, acquireLocksOnSubLinks_context *context)
310{
311	if (node == NULL)
312		return false;
313	if (IsA(node, SubLink))
314	{
315		SubLink    *sub = (SubLink *) node;
316
317		/* Do what we came for */
318		AcquireRewriteLocks((Query *) sub->subselect,
319							context->for_execute,
320							false);
321		/* Fall through to process lefthand args of SubLink */
322	}
323
324	/*
325	 * Do NOT recurse into Query nodes, because AcquireRewriteLocks already
326	 * processed subselects of subselects for us.
327	 */
328	return expression_tree_walker(node, acquireLocksOnSubLinks, context);
329}
330
331
332/*
333 * rewriteRuleAction -
334 *	  Rewrite the rule action with appropriate qualifiers (taken from
335 *	  the triggering query).
336 *
337 * Input arguments:
338 *	parsetree - original query
339 *	rule_action - one action (query) of a rule
340 *	rule_qual - WHERE condition of rule, or NULL if unconditional
341 *	rt_index - RT index of result relation in original query
342 *	event - type of rule event
343 * Output arguments:
344 *	*returning_flag - set true if we rewrite RETURNING clause in rule_action
345 *					(must be initialized to false)
346 * Return value:
347 *	rewritten form of rule_action
348 */
349static Query *
350rewriteRuleAction(Query *parsetree,
351				  Query *rule_action,
352				  Node *rule_qual,
353				  int rt_index,
354				  CmdType event,
355				  bool *returning_flag)
356{
357	int			current_varno,
358				new_varno;
359	int			rt_length;
360	Query	   *sub_action;
361	Query	  **sub_action_ptr;
362	acquireLocksOnSubLinks_context context;
363	ListCell   *lc;
364
365	context.for_execute = true;
366
367	/*
368	 * Make modifiable copies of rule action and qual (what we're passed are
369	 * the stored versions in the relcache; don't touch 'em!).
370	 */
371	rule_action = copyObject(rule_action);
372	rule_qual = copyObject(rule_qual);
373
374	/*
375	 * Acquire necessary locks and fix any deleted JOIN RTE entries.
376	 */
377	AcquireRewriteLocks(rule_action, true, false);
378	(void) acquireLocksOnSubLinks(rule_qual, &context);
379
380	current_varno = rt_index;
381	rt_length = list_length(parsetree->rtable);
382	new_varno = PRS2_NEW_VARNO + rt_length;
383
384	/*
385	 * Adjust rule action and qual to offset its varnos, so that we can merge
386	 * its rtable with the main parsetree's rtable.
387	 *
388	 * If the rule action is an INSERT...SELECT, the OLD/NEW rtable entries
389	 * will be in the SELECT part, and we have to modify that rather than the
390	 * top-level INSERT (kluge!).
391	 */
392	sub_action = getInsertSelectQuery(rule_action, &sub_action_ptr);
393
394	OffsetVarNodes((Node *) sub_action, rt_length, 0);
395	OffsetVarNodes(rule_qual, rt_length, 0);
396	/* but references to OLD should point at original rt_index */
397	ChangeVarNodes((Node *) sub_action,
398				   PRS2_OLD_VARNO + rt_length, rt_index, 0);
399	ChangeVarNodes(rule_qual,
400				   PRS2_OLD_VARNO + rt_length, rt_index, 0);
401
402	/*
403	 * Mark any subquery RTEs in the rule action as LATERAL if they contain
404	 * Vars referring to the current query level (references to NEW/OLD).
405	 * Those really are lateral references, but we've historically not
406	 * required users to mark such subqueries with LATERAL explicitly.  But
407	 * the planner will complain if such Vars exist in a non-LATERAL subquery,
408	 * so we have to fix things up here.
409	 */
410	foreach(lc, sub_action->rtable)
411	{
412		RangeTblEntry *rte = (RangeTblEntry *) lfirst(lc);
413
414		if (rte->rtekind == RTE_SUBQUERY && !rte->lateral &&
415			contain_vars_of_level((Node *) rte->subquery, 1))
416			rte->lateral = true;
417	}
418
419	/*
420	 * Generate expanded rtable consisting of main parsetree's rtable plus
421	 * rule action's rtable; this becomes the complete rtable for the rule
422	 * action.  Some of the entries may be unused after we finish rewriting,
423	 * but we leave them all in place to avoid having to adjust the query's
424	 * varnos.  RT entries that are not referenced in the completed jointree
425	 * will be ignored by the planner, so they do not affect query semantics.
426	 *
427	 * Also merge RTEPermissionInfo lists to ensure that all permissions are
428	 * checked correctly.
429	 *
430	 * If the rule is INSTEAD, then the original query won't be executed at
431	 * all, and so its rteperminfos must be preserved so that the executor
432	 * will do the correct permissions checks on the relations referenced in
433	 * it. This allows us to check that the caller has, say, insert-permission
434	 * on a view, when the view is not semantically referenced at all in the
435	 * resulting query.
436	 *
437	 * When a rule is not INSTEAD, the permissions checks done using the
438	 * copied entries will be redundant with those done during execution of
439	 * the original query, but we don't bother to treat that case differently.
440	 *
441	 * NOTE: because planner will destructively alter rtable and rteperminfos,
442	 * we must ensure that rule action's lists are separate and shares no
443	 * substructure with the main query's lists.  Hence do a deep copy here
444	 * for both.
445	 */
446	{
447		List	   *rtable_tail = sub_action->rtable;
448		List	   *perminfos_tail = sub_action->rteperminfos;
449
450		/*
451		 * RewriteQuery relies on the fact that RT entries from the original
452		 * query appear at the start of the expanded rtable, so we put the
453		 * action's original table at the end of the list.
454		 */
455		sub_action->rtable = copyObject(parsetree->rtable);
456		sub_action->rteperminfos = copyObject(parsetree->rteperminfos);
457		CombineRangeTables(&sub_action->rtable, &sub_action->rteperminfos,
458						   rtable_tail, perminfos_tail);
459	}
460
461	/*
462	 * There could have been some SubLinks in parsetree's rtable, in which
463	 * case we'd better mark the sub_action correctly.
464	 */
465	if (parsetree->hasSubLinks && !sub_action->hasSubLinks)
466	{
467		foreach(lc, parsetree->rtable)
468		{
469			RangeTblEntry *rte = (RangeTblEntry *) lfirst(lc);
470
471			switch (rte->rtekind)
472			{
473				case RTE_RELATION:
474					sub_action->hasSubLinks =
475						checkExprHasSubLink((Node *) rte->tablesample);
476					break;
477				case RTE_FUNCTION:
478					sub_action->hasSubLinks =
479						checkExprHasSubLink((Node *) rte->functions);
480					break;
481				case RTE_TABLEFUNC:
482					sub_action->hasSubLinks =
483						checkExprHasSubLink((Node *) rte->tablefunc);
484					break;
485				case RTE_VALUES:
486					sub_action->hasSubLinks =
487						checkExprHasSubLink((Node *) rte->values_lists);
488					break;
489				default:
490					/* other RTE types don't contain bare expressions */
491					break;
492			}
493			sub_action->hasSubLinks |=
494				checkExprHasSubLink((Node *) rte->securityQuals);
495			if (sub_action->hasSubLinks)
496				break;			/* no need to keep scanning rtable */
497		}
498	}
499
500	/*
501	 * Also, we might have absorbed some RTEs with RLS conditions into the
502	 * sub_action.  If so, mark it as hasRowSecurity, whether or not those
503	 * RTEs will be referenced after we finish rewriting.  (Note: currently
504	 * this is a no-op because RLS conditions aren't added till later, but it
505	 * seems like good future-proofing to do this anyway.)
506	 */
507	sub_action->hasRowSecurity |= parsetree->hasRowSecurity;
508
509	/*
510	 * Each rule action's jointree should be the main parsetree's jointree
511	 * plus that rule's jointree, but usually *without* the original rtindex
512	 * that we're replacing (if present, which it won't be for INSERT). Note
513	 * that if the rule action refers to OLD, its jointree will add a
514	 * reference to rt_index.  If the rule action doesn't refer to OLD, but
515	 * either the rule_qual or the user query quals do, then we need to keep
516	 * the original rtindex in the jointree to provide data for the quals.  We
517	 * don't want the original rtindex to be joined twice, however, so avoid
518	 * keeping it if the rule action mentions it.
519	 *
520	 * As above, the action's jointree must not share substructure with the
521	 * main parsetree's.
522	 */
523	if (sub_action->commandType != CMD_UTILITY)
524	{
525		bool		keeporig;
526		List	   *newjointree;
527
528		Assert(sub_action->jointree != NULL);
529		keeporig = (!rangeTableEntry_used((Node *) sub_action->jointree,
530										  rt_index, 0)) &&
531			(rangeTableEntry_used(rule_qual, rt_index, 0) ||
532			 rangeTableEntry_used(parsetree->jointree->quals, rt_index, 0));
533		newjointree = adjustJoinTreeList(parsetree, !keeporig, rt_index);
534		if (newjointree != NIL)
535		{
536			/*
537			 * If sub_action is a setop, manipulating its jointree will do no
538			 * good at all, because the jointree is dummy.  (Perhaps someday
539			 * we could push the joining and quals down to the member
540			 * statements of the setop?)
541			 */
542			if (sub_action->setOperations != NULL)
543				ereport(ERROR,
544						(errcode(ERRCODE_FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED),
545						 errmsg("conditional UNION/INTERSECT/EXCEPT statements are not implemented")));
546
547			sub_action->jointree->fromlist =
548				list_concat(newjointree, sub_action->jointree->fromlist);
549
550			/*
551			 * There could have been some SubLinks in newjointree, in which
552			 * case we'd better mark the sub_action correctly.
553			 */
554			if (parsetree->hasSubLinks && !sub_action->hasSubLinks)
555				sub_action->hasSubLinks =
556					checkExprHasSubLink((Node *) newjointree);
557		}
558	}
559
560	/*
561	 * If the original query has any CTEs, copy them into the rule action. But
562	 * we don't need them for a utility action.
563	 */
564	if (parsetree->cteList != NIL && sub_action->commandType != CMD_UTILITY)
565	{
566		/*
567		 * Annoying implementation restriction: because CTEs are identified by
568		 * name within a cteList, we can't merge a CTE from the original query
569		 * if it has the same name as any CTE in the rule action.
570		 *
571		 * This could possibly be fixed by using some sort of internally
572		 * generated ID, instead of names, to link CTE RTEs to their CTEs.
573		 * However, decompiling the results would be quite confusing; note the
574		 * merge of hasRecursive flags below, which could change the apparent
575		 * semantics of such redundantly-named CTEs.
576		 */
577		foreach(lc, parsetree->cteList)
578		{
579			CommonTableExpr *cte = (CommonTableExpr *) lfirst(lc);
580			ListCell   *lc2;
581
582			foreach(lc2, sub_action->cteList)
583			{
584				CommonTableExpr *cte2 = (CommonTableExpr *) lfirst(lc2);
585
586				if (strcmp(cte->ctename, cte2->ctename) == 0)
587					ereport(ERROR,
588							(errcode(ERRCODE_FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED),
589							 errmsg("WITH query name \"%s\" appears in both a rule action and the query being rewritten",
590									cte->ctename)));
591			}
592		}
593
594		/*
595		 * OK, it's safe to combine the CTE lists.  Beware that RewriteQuery
596		 * knows we concatenate the lists in this order.
597		 */
598		sub_action->cteList = list_concat(sub_action->cteList,
599										  copyObject(parsetree->cteList));
600		/* ... and don't forget about the associated flags */
601		sub_action->hasRecursive |= parsetree->hasRecursive;
602		sub_action->hasModifyingCTE |= parsetree->hasModifyingCTE;
603
604		/*
605		 * If rule_action is different from sub_action (i.e., the rule action
606		 * is an INSERT...SELECT), then we might have just added some
607		 * data-modifying CTEs that are not at the top query level.  This is
608		 * disallowed by the parser and we mustn't generate such trees here
609		 * either, so throw an error.
610		 *
611		 * Conceivably such cases could be supported by attaching the original
612		 * query's CTEs to rule_action not sub_action.  But to do that, we'd
613		 * have to increment ctelevelsup in RTEs and SubLinks copied from the
614		 * original query.  For now, it doesn't seem worth the trouble.
615		 */
616		if (sub_action->hasModifyingCTE && rule_action != sub_action)
617			ereport(ERROR,
618					(errcode(ERRCODE_FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED),
619					 errmsg("INSERT ... SELECT rule actions are not supported for queries having data-modifying statements in WITH")));
620	}
621
622	/*
623	 * Event Qualification forces copying of parsetree and splitting into two
624	 * queries one w/rule_qual, one w/NOT rule_qual. Also add user query qual
625	 * onto rule action
626	 */
627	AddQual(sub_action, rule_qual);
628
629	AddQual(sub_action, parsetree->jointree->quals);
630
631	/*
632	 * Rewrite new.attribute with right hand side of target-list entry for
633	 * appropriate field name in insert/update.
634	 *
635	 * KLUGE ALERT: since ReplaceVarsFromTargetList returns a mutated copy, we
636	 * can't just apply it to sub_action; we have to remember to update the
637	 * sublink inside rule_action, too.
638	 */
639	if ((event == CMD_INSERT || event == CMD_UPDATE) &&
640		sub_action->commandType != CMD_UTILITY)
641	{
642		RangeTblEntry *new_rte = rt_fetch(new_varno, sub_action->rtable);
643		Relation	new_rel;
644		List	   *gen_cols;
645
646		/*
647		 * The target list does not contain entries for generated columns
648		 * (they are removed by rewriteTargetListIU), so we must build entries
649		 * for them here, so that new.gen_col can be rewritten correctly.
650		 */
651		new_rel = relation_open(new_rte->relid, NoLock);
652		gen_cols = get_generated_columns(new_rel, new_varno, true);
653		relation_close(new_rel, NoLock);
654
655		/*
656		 * The generated column expressions refer to new.attribute, so they
657		 * must be rewritten before they can be used as replacements.
658		 */
659		gen_cols = (List *)
660			ReplaceVarsFromTargetList((Node *) gen_cols,
661									  new_varno,
662									  0,
663									  new_rte,
664									  parsetree->targetList,
665									  sub_action->resultRelation,
666									  (event == CMD_UPDATE) ?
667									  REPLACEVARS_CHANGE_VARNO :
668									  REPLACEVARS_SUBSTITUTE_NULL,
669									  current_varno,
670									  &sub_action->hasSubLinks);
671
672		/*
673		 * Now rewrite new.attribute in sub_action, using both the target list
674		 * and the rewritten generated column expressions.
675		 */
676		sub_action = (Query *)
677			ReplaceVarsFromTargetList((Node *) sub_action,
678									  new_varno,
679									  0,
680									  new_rte,
681									  list_concat(gen_cols, parsetree->targetList),
682									  sub_action->resultRelation,
683									  (event == CMD_UPDATE) ?
684									  REPLACEVARS_CHANGE_VARNO :
685									  REPLACEVARS_SUBSTITUTE_NULL,
686									  current_varno,
687									  NULL);
688		if (sub_action_ptr)
689			*sub_action_ptr = sub_action;
690		else
691			rule_action = sub_action;
692	}
693
694	/*
695	 * If rule_action has a RETURNING clause, then either throw it away if the
696	 * triggering query has no RETURNING clause, or rewrite it to emit what
697	 * the triggering query's RETURNING clause asks for.  Throw an error if
698	 * more than one rule has a RETURNING clause.
699	 */
700	if (!parsetree->returningList)
701		rule_action->returningList = NIL;
702	else if (rule_action->returningList)
703	{
704		if (*returning_flag)
705			ereport(ERROR,
706					(errcode(ERRCODE_FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED),
707					 errmsg("cannot have RETURNING lists in multiple rules")));
708		*returning_flag = true;
709		rule_action->returningList = (List *)
710			ReplaceVarsFromTargetList((Node *) parsetree->returningList,
711									  parsetree->resultRelation,
712									  0,
713									  rt_fetch(parsetree->resultRelation,
714											   parsetree->rtable),
715									  rule_action->returningList,
716									  rule_action->resultRelation,
717									  REPLACEVARS_REPORT_ERROR,
718									  0,
719									  &rule_action->hasSubLinks);
720
721		/* use triggering query's aliases for OLD and NEW in RETURNING list */
722		rule_action->returningOldAlias = parsetree->returningOldAlias;
723		rule_action->returningNewAlias = parsetree->returningNewAlias;
724
725		/*
726		 * There could have been some SubLinks in parsetree's returningList,
727		 * in which case we'd better mark the rule_action correctly.
728		 */
729		if (parsetree->hasSubLinks && !rule_action->hasSubLinks)
730			rule_action->hasSubLinks =
731				checkExprHasSubLink((Node *) rule_action->returningList);
732	}
733
734	return rule_action;
735}
736
737/*
738 * Copy the query's jointree list, and optionally attempt to remove any
739 * occurrence of the given rt_index as a top-level join item (we do not look
740 * for it within join items; this is OK because we are only expecting to find
741 * it as an UPDATE or DELETE target relation, which will be at the top level
742 * of the join).  Returns modified jointree list --- this is a separate copy
743 * sharing no nodes with the original.
744 */
745static List *
746adjustJoinTreeList(Query *parsetree, bool removert, int rt_index)
747{
748	List	   *newjointree = copyObject(parsetree->jointree->fromlist);
749	ListCell   *l;
750
751	if (removert)
752	{
753		foreach(l, newjointree)
754		{
755			RangeTblRef *rtr = lfirst(l);
756
757			if (IsA(rtr, RangeTblRef) &&
758				rtr->rtindex == rt_index)
759			{
760				newjointree = foreach_delete_current(newjointree, l);
761				break;
762			}
763		}
764	}
765	return newjointree;
766}
767
768
769/*
770 * rewriteTargetListIU - rewrite INSERT/UPDATE targetlist into standard form
771 *
772 * This has the following responsibilities:
773 *
774 * 1. For an INSERT, add tlist entries to compute default values for any
775 * attributes that have defaults and are not assigned to in the given tlist.
776 * (We do not insert anything for default-less attributes, however.  The
777 * planner will later insert NULLs for them, but there's no reason to slow
778 * down rewriter processing with extra tlist nodes.)  Also, for both INSERT
779 * and UPDATE, replace explicit DEFAULT specifications with column default
780 * expressions.
781 *
782 * 2. Merge multiple entries for the same target attribute, or declare error
783 * if we can't.  Multiple entries are only allowed for INSERT/UPDATE of
784 * portions of an array or record field, for example
785 *			UPDATE table SET foo[2] = 42, foo[4] = 43;
786 * We can merge such operations into a single assignment op.  Essentially,
787 * the expression we want to produce in this case is like
788 *		foo = array_set_element(array_set_element(foo, 2, 42), 4, 43)
789 *
790 * 3. Sort the tlist into standard order: non-junk fields in order by resno,
791 * then junk fields (these in no particular order).
792 *
793 * We must do items 1 and 2 before firing rewrite rules, else rewritten
794 * references to NEW.foo will produce wrong or incomplete results.  Item 3
795 * is not needed for rewriting, but it is helpful for the planner, and we
796 * can do it essentially for free while handling the other items.
797 *
798 * If values_rte is non-NULL (i.e., we are doing a multi-row INSERT using
799 * values from a VALUES RTE), we populate *unused_values_attrnos with the
800 * attribute numbers of any unused columns from the VALUES RTE.  This can
801 * happen for identity and generated columns whose targetlist entries are
802 * replaced with generated expressions (if INSERT ... OVERRIDING USER VALUE is
803 * used, or all the values to be inserted are DEFAULT).  This information is
804 * required by rewriteValuesRTE() to handle any DEFAULT items in the unused
805 * columns.  The caller must have initialized *unused_values_attrnos to NULL.
806 */
807static List *
808rewriteTargetListIU(List *targetList,
809					CmdType commandType,
810					OverridingKind override,
811					Relation target_relation,
812					RangeTblEntry *values_rte,
813					int values_rte_index,
814					Bitmapset **unused_values_attrnos)
815{
816	TargetEntry **new_tles;
817	List	   *new_tlist = NIL;
818	List	   *junk_tlist = NIL;
819	Form_pg_attribute att_tup;
820	int			attrno,
821				next_junk_attrno,
822				numattrs;
823	ListCell   *temp;
824	Bitmapset  *default_only_cols = NULL;
825
826	/*
827	 * We process the normal (non-junk) attributes by scanning the input tlist
828	 * once and transferring TLEs into an array, then scanning the array to
829	 * build an output tlist.  This avoids O(N^2) behavior for large numbers
830	 * of attributes.
831	 *
832	 * Junk attributes are tossed into a separate list during the same tlist
833	 * scan, then appended to the reconstructed tlist.
834	 */
835	numattrs = RelationGetNumberOfAttributes(target_relation);
836	new_tles = (TargetEntry **) palloc0(numattrs * sizeof(TargetEntry *));
837	next_junk_attrno = numattrs + 1;
838
839	foreach(temp, targetList)
840	{
841		TargetEntry *old_tle = (TargetEntry *) lfirst(temp);
842
843		if (!old_tle->resjunk)
844		{
845			/* Normal attr: stash it into new_tles[] */
846			attrno = old_tle->resno;
847			if (attrno < 1 || attrno > numattrs)
848				elog(ERROR, "bogus resno %d in targetlist", attrno);
849			att_tup = TupleDescAttr(target_relation->rd_att, attrno - 1);
850
851			/* We can (and must) ignore deleted attributes */
852			if (att_tup->attisdropped)
853				continue;
854
855			/* Merge with any prior assignment to same attribute */
856			new_tles[attrno - 1] =
857				process_matched_tle(old_tle,
858									new_tles[attrno - 1],
859									NameStr(att_tup->attname));
860		}
861		else
862		{
863			/*
864			 * Copy all resjunk tlist entries to junk_tlist, and assign them
865			 * resnos above the last real resno.
866			 *
867			 * Typical junk entries include ORDER BY or GROUP BY expressions
868			 * (are these actually possible in an INSERT or UPDATE?), system
869			 * attribute references, etc.
870			 */
871
872			/* Get the resno right, but don't copy unnecessarily */
873			if (old_tle->resno != next_junk_attrno)
874			{
875				old_tle = flatCopyTargetEntry(old_tle);
876				old_tle->resno = next_junk_attrno;
877			}
878			junk_tlist = lappend(junk_tlist, old_tle);
879			next_junk_attrno++;
880		}
881	}
882
883	for (attrno = 1; attrno <= numattrs; attrno++)
884	{
885		TargetEntry *new_tle = new_tles[attrno - 1];
886		bool		apply_default;
887
888		att_tup = TupleDescAttr(target_relation->rd_att, attrno - 1);
889
890		/* We can (and must) ignore deleted attributes */
891		if (att_tup->attisdropped)
892			continue;
893
894		/*
895		 * Handle the two cases where we need to insert a default expression:
896		 * it's an INSERT and there's no tlist entry for the column, or the
897		 * tlist entry is a DEFAULT placeholder node.
898		 */
899		apply_default = ((new_tle == NULL && commandType == CMD_INSERT) ||
900						 (new_tle && new_tle->expr && IsA(new_tle->expr, SetToDefault)));
901
902		if (commandType == CMD_INSERT)
903		{
904			int			values_attrno = 0;
905
906			/* Source attribute number for values that come from a VALUES RTE */
907			if (values_rte && new_tle && IsA(new_tle->expr, Var))
908			{
909				Var		   *var = (Var *) new_tle->expr;
910
911				if (var->varno == values_rte_index)
912					values_attrno = var->varattno;
913			}
914
915			/*
916			 * Can only insert DEFAULT into GENERATED ALWAYS identity columns,
917			 * unless either OVERRIDING USER VALUE or OVERRIDING SYSTEM VALUE
918			 * is specified.
919			 */
920			if (att_tup->attidentity == ATTRIBUTE_IDENTITY_ALWAYS && !apply_default)
921			{
922				if (override == OVERRIDING_USER_VALUE)
923					apply_default = true;
924				else if (override != OVERRIDING_SYSTEM_VALUE)
925				{
926					/*
927					 * If this column's values come from a VALUES RTE, test
928					 * whether it contains only SetToDefault items.  Since the
929					 * VALUES list might be quite large, we arrange to only
930					 * scan it once.
931					 */
932					if (values_attrno != 0)
933					{
934						if (default_only_cols == NULL)
935							default_only_cols = findDefaultOnlyColumns(values_rte);
936
937						if (bms_is_member(values_attrno, default_only_cols))
938							apply_default = true;
939					}
940
941					if (!apply_default)
942						ereport(ERROR,
943								(errcode(ERRCODE_GENERATED_ALWAYS),
944								 errmsg("cannot insert a non-DEFAULT value into column \"%s\"",
945										NameStr(att_tup->attname)),
946								 errdetail("Column \"%s\" is an identity column defined as GENERATED ALWAYS.",
947										   NameStr(att_tup->attname)),
948								 errhint("Use OVERRIDING SYSTEM VALUE to override.")));
949				}
950			}
951
952			/*
953			 * Although inserting into a GENERATED BY DEFAULT identity column
954			 * is allowed, apply the default if OVERRIDING USER VALUE is
955			 * specified.
956			 */
957			if (att_tup->attidentity == ATTRIBUTE_IDENTITY_BY_DEFAULT &&
958				override == OVERRIDING_USER_VALUE)
959				apply_default = true;
960
961			/*
962			 * Can only insert DEFAULT into generated columns, regardless of
963			 * any OVERRIDING clauses.
964			 */
965			if (att_tup->attgenerated && !apply_default)
966			{
967				/*
968				 * If this column's values come from a VALUES RTE, test
969				 * whether it contains only SetToDefault items, as above.
970				 */
971				if (values_attrno != 0)
972				{
973					if (default_only_cols == NULL)
974						default_only_cols = findDefaultOnlyColumns(values_rte);
975
976					if (bms_is_member(values_attrno, default_only_cols))
977						apply_default = true;
978				}
979
980				if (!apply_default)
981					ereport(ERROR,
982							(errcode(ERRCODE_GENERATED_ALWAYS),
983							 errmsg("cannot insert a non-DEFAULT value into column \"%s\"",
984									NameStr(att_tup->attname)),
985							 errdetail("Column \"%s\" is a generated column.",
986									   NameStr(att_tup->attname))));
987			}
988
989			/*
990			 * For an INSERT from a VALUES RTE, return the attribute numbers
991			 * of any VALUES columns that will no longer be used (due to the
992			 * targetlist entry being replaced by a default expression).
993			 */
994			if (values_attrno != 0 && apply_default && unused_values_attrnos)
995				*unused_values_attrnos = bms_add_member(*unused_values_attrnos,
996														values_attrno);
997		}
998
999		/*
1000		 * Updates to identity and generated columns follow the same rules as
1001		 * above, except that UPDATE doesn't admit OVERRIDING clauses.  Also,
1002		 * the source can't be a VALUES RTE, so we needn't consider that.
1003		 */
1004		if (commandType == CMD_UPDATE)
1005		{
1006			if (att_tup->attidentity == ATTRIBUTE_IDENTITY_ALWAYS &&
1007				new_tle && !apply_default)
1008				ereport(ERROR,
1009						(errcode(ERRCODE_GENERATED_ALWAYS),
1010						 errmsg("column \"%s\" can only be updated to DEFAULT",
1011								NameStr(att_tup->attname)),
1012						 errdetail("Column \"%s\" is an identity column defined as GENERATED ALWAYS.",
1013								   NameStr(att_tup->attname))));
1014
1015			if (att_tup->attgenerated && new_tle && !apply_default)
1016				ereport(ERROR,
1017						(errcode(ERRCODE_GENERATED_ALWAYS),
1018						 errmsg("column \"%s\" can only be updated to DEFAULT",
1019								NameStr(att_tup->attname)),
1020						 errdetail("Column \"%s\" is a generated column.",
1021								   NameStr(att_tup->attname))));
1022		}
1023
1024		if (att_tup->attgenerated)
1025		{
1026			/*
1027			 * virtual generated column stores a null value; stored generated
1028			 * column will be fixed in executor
1029			 */
1030			new_tle = NULL;
1031		}
1032		else if (apply_default)
1033		{
1034			Node	   *new_expr;
1035
1036			new_expr = build_column_default(target_relation, attrno);
1037
1038			/*
1039			 * If there is no default (ie, default is effectively NULL), we
1040			 * can omit the tlist entry in the INSERT case, since the planner
1041			 * can insert a NULL for itself, and there's no point in spending
1042			 * any more rewriter cycles on the entry.  But in the UPDATE case
1043			 * we've got to explicitly set the column to NULL.
1044			 */
1045			if (!new_expr)
1046			{
1047				if (commandType == CMD_INSERT)
1048					new_tle = NULL;
1049				else
1050					new_expr = coerce_null_to_domain(att_tup->atttypid,
1051													 att_tup->atttypmod,
1052													 att_tup->attcollation,
1053													 att_tup->attlen,
1054													 att_tup->attbyval);
1055			}
1056
1057			if (new_expr)
1058				new_tle = makeTargetEntry((Expr *) new_expr,
1059										  attrno,
1060										  pstrdup(NameStr(att_tup->attname)),
1061										  false);
1062		}
1063
1064		if (new_tle)
1065			new_tlist = lappend(new_tlist, new_tle);
1066	}
1067
1068	pfree(new_tles);
1069
1070	return list_concat(new_tlist, junk_tlist);
1071}
1072
1073
1074/*
1075 * Convert a matched TLE from the original tlist into a correct new TLE.
1076 *
1077 * This routine detects and handles multiple assignments to the same target
1078 * attribute.  (The attribute name is needed only for error messages.)
1079 */
1080static TargetEntry *
1081process_matched_tle(TargetEntry *src_tle,
1082					TargetEntry *prior_tle,
1083					const char *attrName)
1084{
1085	TargetEntry *result;
1086	CoerceToDomain *coerce_expr = NULL;
1087	Node	   *src_expr;
1088	Node	   *prior_expr;
1089	Node	   *src_input;
1090	Node	   *prior_input;
1091	Node	   *priorbottom;
1092	Node	   *newexpr;
1093
1094	if (prior_tle == NULL)
1095	{
1096		/*
1097		 * Normal case where this is the first assignment to the attribute.
1098		 */
1099		return src_tle;
1100	}
1101
1102	/*----------
1103	 * Multiple assignments to same attribute.  Allow only if all are
1104	 * FieldStore or SubscriptingRef assignment operations.  This is a bit
1105	 * tricky because what we may actually be looking at is a nest of
1106	 * such nodes; consider
1107	 *		UPDATE tab SET col.fld1.subfld1 = x, col.fld2.subfld2 = y
1108	 * The two expressions produced by the parser will look like
1109	 *		FieldStore(col, fld1, FieldStore(placeholder, subfld1, x))
1110	 *		FieldStore(col, fld2, FieldStore(placeholder, subfld2, y))
1111	 * However, we can ignore the substructure and just consider the top
1112	 * FieldStore or SubscriptingRef from each assignment, because it works to
1113	 * combine these as
1114	 *		FieldStore(FieldStore(col, fld1,
1115	 *							  FieldStore(placeholder, subfld1, x)),
1116	 *				   fld2, FieldStore(placeholder, subfld2, y))
1117	 * Note the leftmost expression goes on the inside so that the
1118	 * assignments appear to occur left-to-right.
1119	 *
1120	 * For FieldStore, instead of nesting we can generate a single
1121	 * FieldStore with multiple target fields.  We must nest when
1122	 * SubscriptingRefs are involved though.
1123	 *
1124	 * As a further complication, the destination column might be a domain,
1125	 * resulting in each assignment containing a CoerceToDomain node over a
1126	 * FieldStore or SubscriptingRef.  These should have matching target
1127	 * domains, so we strip them and reconstitute a single CoerceToDomain over
1128	 * the combined FieldStore/SubscriptingRef nodes.  (Notice that this has
1129	 * the result that the domain's checks are applied only after we do all
1130	 * the field or element updates, not after each one.  This is desirable.)
1131	 *----------
1132	 */
1133	src_expr = (Node *) src_tle->expr;
1134	prior_expr = (Node *) prior_tle->expr;
1135
1136	if (src_expr && IsA(src_expr, CoerceToDomain) &&
1137		prior_expr && IsA(prior_expr, CoerceToDomain) &&
1138		((CoerceToDomain *) src_expr)->resulttype ==
1139		((CoerceToDomain *) prior_expr)->resulttype)
1140	{
1141		/* we assume without checking that resulttypmod/resultcollid match */
1142		coerce_expr = (CoerceToDomain *) src_expr;
1143		src_expr = (Node *) ((CoerceToDomain *) src_expr)->arg;
1144		prior_expr = (Node *) ((CoerceToDomain *) prior_expr)->arg;
1145	}
1146
1147	src_input = get_assignment_input(src_expr);
1148	prior_input = get_assignment_input(prior_expr);
1149	if (src_input == NULL ||
1150		prior_input == NULL ||
1151		exprType(src_expr) != exprType(prior_expr))
1152		ereport(ERROR,
1153				(errcode(ERRCODE_SYNTAX_ERROR),
1154				 errmsg("multiple assignments to same column \"%s\"",
1155						attrName)));
1156
1157	/*
1158	 * Prior TLE could be a nest of assignments if we do this more than once.
1159	 */
1160	priorbottom = prior_input;
1161	for (;;)
1162	{
1163		Node	   *newbottom = get_assignment_input(priorbottom);
1164
1165		if (newbottom == NULL)
1166			break;				/* found the original Var reference */
1167		priorbottom = newbottom;
1168	}
1169	if (!equal(priorbottom, src_input))
1170		ereport(ERROR,
1171				(errcode(ERRCODE_SYNTAX_ERROR),
1172				 errmsg("multiple assignments to same column \"%s\"",
1173						attrName)));
1174
1175	/*
1176	 * Looks OK to nest 'em.
1177	 */
1178	if (IsA(src_expr, FieldStore))
1179	{
1180		FieldStore *fstore = makeNode(FieldStore);
1181
1182		if (IsA(prior_expr, FieldStore))
1183		{
1184			/* combine the two */
1185			memcpy(fstore, prior_expr, sizeof(FieldStore));
1186			fstore->newvals =
1187				list_concat_copy(((FieldStore *) prior_expr)->newvals,
1188								 ((FieldStore *) src_expr)->newvals);
1189			fstore->fieldnums =
1190				list_concat_copy(((FieldStore *) prior_expr)->fieldnums,
1191								 ((FieldStore *) src_expr)->fieldnums);
1192		}
1193		else
1194		{
1195			/* general case, just nest 'em */
1196			memcpy(fstore, src_expr, sizeof(FieldStore));
1197			fstore->arg = (Expr *) prior_expr;
1198		}
1199		newexpr = (Node *) fstore;
1200	}
1201	else if (IsA(src_expr, SubscriptingRef))
1202	{
1203		SubscriptingRef *sbsref = makeNode(SubscriptingRef);
1204
1205		memcpy(sbsref, src_expr, sizeof(SubscriptingRef));
1206		sbsref->refexpr = (Expr *) prior_expr;
1207		newexpr = (Node *) sbsref;
1208	}
1209	else
1210	{
1211		elog(ERROR, "cannot happen");
1212		newexpr = NULL;
1213	}
1214
1215	if (coerce_expr)
1216	{
1217		/* put back the CoerceToDomain */
1218		CoerceToDomain *newcoerce = makeNode(CoerceToDomain);
1219
1220		memcpy(newcoerce, coerce_expr, sizeof(CoerceToDomain));
1221		newcoerce->arg = (Expr *) newexpr;
1222		newexpr = (Node *) newcoerce;
1223	}
1224
1225	result = flatCopyTargetEntry(src_tle);
1226	result->expr = (Expr *) newexpr;
1227	return result;
1228}
1229
1230/*
1231 * If node is an assignment node, return its input; else return NULL
1232 */
1233static Node *
1234get_assignment_input(Node *node)
1235{
1236	if (node == NULL)
1237		return NULL;
1238	if (IsA(node, FieldStore))
1239	{
1240		FieldStore *fstore = (FieldStore *) node;
1241
1242		return (Node *) fstore->arg;
1243	}
1244	else if (IsA(node, SubscriptingRef))
1245	{
1246		SubscriptingRef *sbsref = (SubscriptingRef *) node;
1247
1248		if (sbsref->refassgnexpr == NULL)
1249			return NULL;
1250
1251		return (Node *) sbsref->refexpr;
1252	}
1253
1254	return NULL;
1255}
1256
1257/*
1258 * Make an expression tree for the default value for a column.
1259 *
1260 * If there is no default, return a NULL instead.
1261 */
1262Node *
1263build_column_default(Relation rel, int attrno)
1264{
1265	TupleDesc	rd_att = rel->rd_att;
1266	Form_pg_attribute att_tup = TupleDescAttr(rd_att, attrno - 1);
1267	Oid			atttype = att_tup->atttypid;
1268	int32		atttypmod = att_tup->atttypmod;
1269	Node	   *expr = NULL;
1270	Oid			exprtype;
1271
1272	if (att_tup->attidentity)
1273	{
1274		NextValueExpr *nve = makeNode(NextValueExpr);
1275
1276		nve->seqid = getIdentitySequence(rel, attrno, false);
1277		nve->typeId = att_tup->atttypid;
1278
1279		return (Node *) nve;
1280	}
1281
1282	/*
1283	 * If relation has a default for this column, fetch that expression.
1284	 */
1285	if (att_tup->atthasdef)
1286	{
1287		expr = TupleDescGetDefault(rd_att, attrno);
1288		if (expr == NULL)
1289			elog(ERROR, "default expression not found for attribute %d of relation \"%s\"",
1290				 attrno, RelationGetRelationName(rel));
1291	}
1292
1293	/*
1294	 * No per-column default, so look for a default for the type itself.  But
1295	 * not for generated columns.
1296	 */
1297	if (expr == NULL && !att_tup->attgenerated)
1298		expr = get_typdefault(atttype);
1299
1300	if (expr == NULL)
1301		return NULL;			/* No default anywhere */
1302
1303	/*
1304	 * Make sure the value is coerced to the target column type; this will
1305	 * generally be true already, but there seem to be some corner cases
1306	 * involving domain defaults where it might not be true. This should match
1307	 * the parser's processing of non-defaulted expressions --- see
1308	 * transformAssignedExpr().
1309	 */
1310	exprtype = exprType(expr);
1311
1312	expr = coerce_to_target_type(NULL,	/* no UNKNOWN params here */
1313								 expr, exprtype,
1314								 atttype, atttypmod,
1315								 COERCION_ASSIGNMENT,
1316								 COERCE_IMPLICIT_CAST,
1317								 -1);
1318	if (expr == NULL)
1319		ereport(ERROR,
1320				(errcode(ERRCODE_DATATYPE_MISMATCH),
1321				 errmsg("column \"%s\" is of type %s"
1322						" but default expression is of type %s",
1323						NameStr(att_tup->attname),
1324						format_type_be(atttype),
1325						format_type_be(exprtype)),
1326				 errhint("You will need to rewrite or cast the expression.")));
1327
1328	return expr;
1329}
1330
1331
1332/* Does VALUES RTE contain any SetToDefault items? */
1333static bool
1334searchForDefault(RangeTblEntry *rte)
1335{
1336	ListCell   *lc;
1337
1338	foreach(lc, rte->values_lists)
1339	{
1340		List	   *sublist = (List *) lfirst(lc);
1341		ListCell   *lc2;
1342
1343		foreach(lc2, sublist)
1344		{
1345			Node	   *col = (Node *) lfirst(lc2);
1346
1347			if (IsA(col, SetToDefault))
1348				return true;
1349		}
1350	}
1351	return false;
1352}
1353
1354
1355/*
1356 * Search a VALUES RTE for columns that contain only SetToDefault items,
1357 * returning a Bitmapset containing the attribute numbers of any such columns.
1358 */
1359static Bitmapset *
1360findDefaultOnlyColumns(RangeTblEntry *rte)
1361{
1362	Bitmapset  *default_only_cols = NULL;
1363	ListCell   *lc;
1364
1365	foreach(lc, rte->values_lists)
1366	{
1367		List	   *sublist = (List *) lfirst(lc);
1368		ListCell   *lc2;
1369		int			i;
1370
1371		if (default_only_cols == NULL)
1372		{
1373			/* Populate the initial result bitmap from the first row */
1374			i = 0;
1375			foreach(lc2, sublist)
1376			{
1377				Node	   *col = (Node *) lfirst(lc2);
1378
1379				i++;
1380				if (IsA(col, SetToDefault))
1381					default_only_cols = bms_add_member(default_only_cols, i);
1382			}
1383		}
1384		else
1385		{
1386			/* Update the result bitmap from this next row */
1387			i = 0;
1388			foreach(lc2, sublist)
1389			{
1390				Node	   *col = (Node *) lfirst(lc2);
1391
1392				i++;
1393				if (!IsA(col, SetToDefault))
1394					default_only_cols = bms_del_member(default_only_cols, i);
1395			}
1396		}
1397
1398		/*
1399		 * If no column in the rows read so far contains only DEFAULT items,
1400		 * we are done.
1401		 */
1402		if (bms_is_empty(default_only_cols))
1403			break;
1404	}
1405
1406	return default_only_cols;
1407}
1408
1409
1410/*
1411 * When processing INSERT ... VALUES with a VALUES RTE (ie, multiple VALUES
1412 * lists), we have to replace any DEFAULT items in the VALUES lists with
1413 * the appropriate default expressions.  The other aspects of targetlist
1414 * rewriting need be applied only to the query's targetlist proper.
1415 *
1416 * For an auto-updatable view, each DEFAULT item in the VALUES list is
1417 * replaced with the default from the view, if it has one.  Otherwise it is
1418 * left untouched so that the underlying base relation's default can be
1419 * applied instead (when we later recurse to here after rewriting the query
1420 * to refer to the base relation instead of the view).
1421 *
1422 * For other types of relation, including rule- and trigger-updatable views,
1423 * all DEFAULT items are replaced, and if the target relation doesn't have a
1424 * default, the value is explicitly set to NULL.
1425 *
1426 * Also, if a DEFAULT item is found in a column mentioned in unused_cols,
1427 * it is explicitly set to NULL.  This happens for columns in the VALUES RTE
1428 * whose corresponding targetlist entries have already been replaced with the
1429 * relation's default expressions, so that any values in those columns of the
1430 * VALUES RTE are no longer used.  This can happen for identity and generated
1431 * columns (if INSERT ... OVERRIDING USER VALUE is used, or all the values to
1432 * be inserted are DEFAULT).  In principle we could replace all entries in
1433 * such a column with NULL, whether DEFAULT or not; but it doesn't seem worth
1434 * the trouble.
1435 *
1436 * Note that we may have subscripted or field assignment targetlist entries,
1437 * as well as more complex expressions from already-replaced DEFAULT items if
1438 * we have recursed to here for an auto-updatable view. However, it ought to
1439 * be impossible for such entries to have DEFAULTs assigned to them, except
1440 * for unused columns, as described above --- we should only have to replace
1441 * DEFAULT items for targetlist entries that contain simple Vars referencing
1442 * the VALUES RTE, or which are no longer referred to by the targetlist.
1443 *
1444 * Returns true if all DEFAULT items were replaced, and false if some were
1445 * left untouched.
1446 */
1447static bool
1448rewriteValuesRTE(Query *parsetree, RangeTblEntry *rte, int rti,
1449				 Relation target_relation,
1450				 Bitmapset *unused_cols)
1451{
1452	List	   *newValues;
1453	ListCell   *lc;
1454	bool		isAutoUpdatableView;
1455	bool		allReplaced;
1456	int			numattrs;
1457	int		   *attrnos;
1458
1459	/* Steps below are not sensible for non-INSERT queries */
1460	Assert(parsetree->commandType == CMD_INSERT);
1461	Assert(rte->rtekind == RTE_VALUES);
1462
1463	/*
1464	 * Rebuilding all the lists is a pretty expensive proposition in a big
1465	 * VALUES list, and it's a waste of time if there aren't any DEFAULT
1466	 * placeholders.  So first scan to see if there are any.
1467	 */
1468	if (!searchForDefault(rte))
1469		return true;			/* nothing to do */
1470
1471	/*
1472	 * Scan the targetlist for entries referring to the VALUES RTE, and note
1473	 * the target attributes. As noted above, we should only need to do this
1474	 * for targetlist entries containing simple Vars --- nothing else in the
1475	 * VALUES RTE should contain DEFAULT items (except possibly for unused
1476	 * columns), and we complain if such a thing does occur.
1477	 */
1478	numattrs = list_length(linitial(rte->values_lists));
1479	attrnos = (int *) palloc0(numattrs * sizeof(int));
1480
1481	foreach(lc, parsetree->targetList)
1482	{
1483		TargetEntry *tle = (TargetEntry *) lfirst(lc);
1484
1485		if (IsA(tle->expr, Var))
1486		{
1487			Var		   *var = (Var *) tle->expr;
1488
1489			if (var->varno == rti)
1490			{
1491				int			attrno = var->varattno;
1492
1493				Assert(attrno >= 1 && attrno <= numattrs);
1494				attrnos[attrno - 1] = tle->resno;
1495			}
1496		}
1497	}
1498
1499	/*
1500	 * Check if the target relation is an auto-updatable view, in which case
1501	 * unresolved defaults will be left untouched rather than being set to
1502	 * NULL.
1503	 */
1504	isAutoUpdatableView = false;
1505	if (target_relation->rd_rel->relkind == RELKIND_VIEW &&
1506		!view_has_instead_trigger(target_relation, CMD_INSERT, NIL))
1507	{
1508		List	   *locks;
1509		bool		hasUpdate;
1510		bool		found;
1511		ListCell   *l;
1512
1513		/* Look for an unconditional DO INSTEAD rule */
1514		locks = matchLocks(CMD_INSERT, target_relation,
1515						   parsetree->resultRelation, parsetree, &hasUpdate);
1516
1517		found = false;
1518		foreach(l, locks)
1519		{
1520			RewriteRule *rule_lock = (RewriteRule *) lfirst(l);
1521
1522			if (rule_lock->isInstead &&
1523				rule_lock->qual == NULL)
1524			{
1525				found = true;
1526				break;
1527			}
1528		}
1529
1530		/*
1531		 * If we didn't find an unconditional DO INSTEAD rule, assume that the
1532		 * view is auto-updatable.  If it isn't, rewriteTargetView() will
1533		 * throw an error.
1534		 */
1535		if (!found)
1536			isAutoUpdatableView = true;
1537	}
1538
1539	newValues = NIL;
1540	allReplaced = true;
1541	foreach(lc, rte->values_lists)
1542	{
1543		List	   *sublist = (List *) lfirst(lc);
1544		List	   *newList = NIL;
1545		ListCell   *lc2;
1546		int			i;
1547
1548		Assert(list_length(sublist) == numattrs);
1549
1550		i = 0;
1551		foreach(lc2, sublist)
1552		{
1553			Node	   *col = (Node *) lfirst(lc2);
1554			int			attrno = attrnos[i++];
1555
1556			if (IsA(col, SetToDefault))
1557			{
1558				Form_pg_attribute att_tup;
1559				Node	   *new_expr;
1560
1561				/*
1562				 * If this column isn't used, just replace the DEFAULT with
1563				 * NULL (attrno will be 0 in this case because the targetlist
1564				 * entry will have been replaced by the default expression).
1565				 */
1566				if (bms_is_member(i, unused_cols))
1567				{
1568					SetToDefault *def = (SetToDefault *) col;
1569
1570					newList = lappend(newList,
1571									  makeNullConst(def->typeId,
1572													def->typeMod,
1573													def->collation));
1574					continue;
1575				}
1576
1577				if (attrno == 0)
1578					elog(ERROR, "cannot set value in column %d to DEFAULT", i);
1579				Assert(attrno > 0 && attrno <= target_relation->rd_att->natts);
1580				att_tup = TupleDescAttr(target_relation->rd_att, attrno - 1);
1581
1582				if (!att_tup->attisdropped)
1583					new_expr = build_column_default(target_relation, attrno);
1584				else
1585					new_expr = NULL;	/* force a NULL if dropped */
1586
1587				/*
1588				 * If there is no default (ie, default is effectively NULL),
1589				 * we've got to explicitly set the column to NULL, unless the
1590				 * target relation is an auto-updatable view.
1591				 */
1592				if (!new_expr)
1593				{
1594					if (isAutoUpdatableView)
1595					{
1596						/* Leave the value untouched */
1597						newList = lappend(newList, col);
1598						allReplaced = false;
1599						continue;
1600					}
1601
1602					new_expr = coerce_null_to_domain(att_tup->atttypid,
1603													 att_tup->atttypmod,
1604													 att_tup->attcollation,
1605													 att_tup->attlen,
1606													 att_tup->attbyval);
1607				}
1608				newList = lappend(newList, new_expr);
1609			}
1610			else
1611				newList = lappend(newList, col);
1612		}
1613		newValues = lappend(newValues, newList);
1614	}
1615	rte->values_lists = newValues;
1616
1617	pfree(attrnos);
1618
1619	return allReplaced;
1620}
1621
1622/*
1623 * Mop up any remaining DEFAULT items in the given VALUES RTE by
1624 * replacing them with NULL constants.
1625 *
1626 * This is used for the product queries generated by DO ALSO rules attached to
1627 * an auto-updatable view.  The action can't depend on the "target relation"
1628 * since the product query might not have one (it needn't be an INSERT).
1629 * Essentially, such queries are treated as being attached to a rule-updatable
1630 * view.
1631 */
1632static void
1633rewriteValuesRTEToNulls(Query *parsetree, RangeTblEntry *rte)
1634{
1635	List	   *newValues;
1636	ListCell   *lc;
1637
1638	newValues = NIL;
1639	foreach(lc, rte->values_lists)
1640	{
1641		List	   *sublist = (List *) lfirst(lc);
1642		List	   *newList = NIL;
1643		ListCell   *lc2;
1644
1645		foreach(lc2, sublist)
1646		{
1647			Node	   *col = (Node *) lfirst(lc2);
1648
1649			if (IsA(col, SetToDefault))
1650			{
1651				SetToDefault *def = (SetToDefault *) col;
1652
1653				newList = lappend(newList, makeNullConst(def->typeId,
1654														 def->typeMod,
1655														 def->collation));
1656			}
1657			else
1658				newList = lappend(newList, col);
1659		}
1660		newValues = lappend(newValues, newList);
1661	}
1662	rte->values_lists = newValues;
1663}
1664
1665
1666/*
1667 * matchLocks -
1668 *	  match a relation's list of locks and returns the matching rules
1669 */
1670static List *
1671matchLocks(CmdType event,
1672		   Relation relation,
1673		   int varno,
1674		   Query *parsetree,
1675		   bool *hasUpdate)
1676{
1677	RuleLock   *rulelocks = relation->rd_rules;
1678	List	   *matching_locks = NIL;
1679	int			nlocks;
1680	int			i;
1681
1682	if (rulelocks == NULL)
1683		return NIL;
1684
1685	if (parsetree->commandType != CMD_SELECT)
1686	{
1687		if (parsetree->resultRelation != varno)
1688			return NIL;
1689	}
1690
1691	nlocks = rulelocks->numLocks;
1692
1693	for (i = 0; i < nlocks; i++)
1694	{
1695		RewriteRule *oneLock = rulelocks->rules[i];
1696
1697		if (oneLock->event == CMD_UPDATE)
1698			*hasUpdate = true;
1699
1700		/*
1701		 * Suppress ON INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE rules that are disabled or
1702		 * configured to not fire during the current session's replication
1703		 * role. ON SELECT rules will always be applied in order to keep views
1704		 * working even in LOCAL or REPLICA role.
1705		 */
1706		if (oneLock->event != CMD_SELECT)
1707		{
1708			if (SessionReplicationRole == SESSION_REPLICATION_ROLE_REPLICA)
1709			{
1710				if (oneLock->enabled == RULE_FIRES_ON_ORIGIN ||
1711					oneLock->enabled == RULE_DISABLED)
1712					continue;
1713			}
1714			else				/* ORIGIN or LOCAL ROLE */
1715			{
1716				if (oneLock->enabled == RULE_FIRES_ON_REPLICA ||
1717					oneLock->enabled == RULE_DISABLED)
1718					continue;
1719			}
1720
1721			/* Non-SELECT rules are not supported for MERGE */
1722			if (parsetree->commandType == CMD_MERGE)
1723				ereport(ERROR,
1724						errcode(ERRCODE_FEATURE_NOT_SUPPORTED),
1725						errmsg("cannot execute MERGE on relation \"%s\"",
1726							   RelationGetRelationName(relation)),
1727						errdetail("MERGE is not supported for relations with rules."));
1728		}
1729
1730		if (oneLock->event == event)
1731		{
1732			if (parsetree->commandType != CMD_SELECT ||
1733				rangeTableEntry_used((Node *) parsetree, varno, 0))
1734				matching_locks = lappend(matching_locks, oneLock);
1735		}
1736	}
1737
1738	return matching_locks;
1739}
1740
1741
1742/*
1743 * ApplyRetrieveRule - expand an ON SELECT rule
1744 */
1745static Query *
1746ApplyRetrieveRule(Query *parsetree,
1747				  RewriteRule *rule,
1748				  int rt_index,
1749				  Relation relation,
1750				  List *activeRIRs)
1751{
1752	Query	   *rule_action;
1753	RangeTblEntry *rte;
1754	RowMarkClause *rc;
1755	int			numCols;
1756
1757	if (list_length(rule->actions) != 1)
1758		elog(ERROR, "expected just one rule action");
1759	if (rule->qual != NULL)
1760		elog(ERROR, "cannot handle qualified ON SELECT rule");
1761
1762	/* Check if the expansion of non-system views are restricted */
1763	if (unlikely((restrict_nonsystem_relation_kind & RESTRICT_RELKIND_VIEW) != 0 &&
1764				 RelationGetRelid(relation) >= FirstNormalObjectId))
1765		ereport(ERROR,
1766				(errcode(ERRCODE_OBJECT_NOT_IN_PREREQUISITE_STATE),
1767				 errmsg("access to non-system view \"%s\" is restricted",
1768						RelationGetRelationName(relation))));
1769
1770	if (rt_index == parsetree->resultRelation)
1771	{
1772		/*
1773		 * We have a view as the result relation of the query, and it wasn't
1774		 * rewritten by any rule.  This case is supported if there is an
1775		 * INSTEAD OF trigger that will trap attempts to insert/update/delete
1776		 * view rows.  The executor will check that; for the moment just plow
1777		 * ahead.  We have two cases:
1778		 *
1779		 * For INSERT, we needn't do anything.  The unmodified RTE will serve
1780		 * fine as the result relation.
1781		 *
1782		 * For UPDATE/DELETE/MERGE, we need to expand the view so as to have
1783		 * source data for the operation.  But we also need an unmodified RTE
1784		 * to serve as the target.  So, copy the RTE and add the copy to the
1785		 * rangetable.  Note that the copy does not get added to the jointree.
1786		 * Also note that there's a hack in fireRIRrules to avoid calling this
1787		 * function again when it arrives at the copied RTE.
1788		 */
1789		if (parsetree->commandType == CMD_INSERT)
1790			return parsetree;
1791		else if (parsetree->commandType == CMD_UPDATE ||
1792				 parsetree->commandType == CMD_DELETE ||
1793				 parsetree->commandType == CMD_MERGE)
1794		{
1795			RangeTblEntry *newrte;
1796			Var		   *var;
1797			TargetEntry *tle;
1798
1799			rte = rt_fetch(rt_index, parsetree->rtable);
1800			newrte = copyObject(rte);
1801			parsetree->rtable = lappend(parsetree->rtable, newrte);
1802			parsetree->resultRelation = list_length(parsetree->rtable);
1803			/* parsetree->mergeTargetRelation unchanged (use expanded view) */
1804
1805			/*
1806			 * For the most part, Vars referencing the view should remain as
1807			 * they are, meaning that they implicitly represent OLD values.
1808			 * But in the RETURNING list if any, we want such Vars to
1809			 * represent NEW values, so change them to reference the new RTE.
1810			 *
1811			 * Since ChangeVarNodes scribbles on the tree in-place, copy the
1812			 * RETURNING list first for safety.
1813			 */
1814			parsetree->returningList = copyObject(parsetree->returningList);
1815			ChangeVarNodes((Node *) parsetree->returningList, rt_index,
1816						   parsetree->resultRelation, 0);
1817
1818			/*
1819			 * To allow the executor to compute the original view row to pass
1820			 * to the INSTEAD OF trigger, we add a resjunk whole-row Var
1821			 * referencing the original RTE.  This will later get expanded
1822			 * into a RowExpr computing all the OLD values of the view row.
1823			 */
1824			var = makeWholeRowVar(rte, rt_index, 0, false);
1825			tle = makeTargetEntry((Expr *) var,
1826								  list_length(parsetree->targetList) + 1,
1827								  pstrdup("wholerow"),
1828								  true);
1829
1830			parsetree->targetList = lappend(parsetree->targetList, tle);
1831
1832			/* Now, continue with expanding the original view RTE */
1833		}
1834		else
1835			elog(ERROR, "unrecognized commandType: %d",
1836				 (int) parsetree->commandType);
1837	}
1838
1839	/*
1840	 * Check if there's a FOR [KEY] UPDATE/SHARE clause applying to this view.
1841	 *
1842	 * Note: we needn't explicitly consider any such clauses appearing in
1843	 * ancestor query levels; their effects have already been pushed down to
1844	 * here by markQueryForLocking, and will be reflected in "rc".
1845	 */
1846	rc = get_parse_rowmark(parsetree, rt_index);
1847
1848	/*
1849	 * Make a modifiable copy of the view query, and acquire needed locks on
1850	 * the relations it mentions.  Force at least RowShareLock for all such
1851	 * rels if there's a FOR [KEY] UPDATE/SHARE clause affecting this view.
1852	 */
1853	rule_action = copyObject(linitial(rule->actions));
1854
1855	AcquireRewriteLocks(rule_action, true, (rc != NULL));
1856
1857	/*
1858	 * If FOR [KEY] UPDATE/SHARE of view, mark all the contained tables as
1859	 * implicit FOR [KEY] UPDATE/SHARE, the same as the parser would have done
1860	 * if the view's subquery had been written out explicitly.
1861	 */
1862	if (rc != NULL)
1863		markQueryForLocking(rule_action, (Node *) rule_action->jointree,
1864							rc->strength, rc->waitPolicy, true);
1865
1866	/*
1867	 * Recursively expand any view references inside the view.
1868	 */
1869	rule_action = fireRIRrules(rule_action, activeRIRs);
1870
1871	/*
1872	 * Make sure the query is marked as having row security if the view query
1873	 * does.
1874	 */
1875	parsetree->hasRowSecurity |= rule_action->hasRowSecurity;
1876
1877	/*
1878	 * Now, plug the view query in as a subselect, converting the relation's
1879	 * original RTE to a subquery RTE.
1880	 */
1881	rte = rt_fetch(rt_index, parsetree->rtable);
1882
1883	rte->rtekind = RTE_SUBQUERY;
1884	rte->subquery = rule_action;
1885	rte->security_barrier = RelationIsSecurityView(relation);
1886
1887	/*
1888	 * Clear fields that should not be set in a subquery RTE.  Note that we
1889	 * leave the relid, relkind, rellockmode, and perminfoindex fields set, so
1890	 * that the view relation can be appropriately locked before execution and
1891	 * its permissions checked.
1892	 */
1893	rte->tablesample = NULL;
1894	rte->inh = false;			/* must not be set for a subquery */
1895
1896	/*
1897	 * Since we allow CREATE OR REPLACE VIEW to add columns to a view, the
1898	 * rule_action might emit more columns than we expected when the current
1899	 * query was parsed.  Various places expect rte->eref->colnames to be
1900	 * consistent with the non-junk output columns of the subquery, so patch
1901	 * things up if necessary by adding some dummy column names.
1902	 */
1903	numCols = ExecCleanTargetListLength(rule_action->targetList);
1904	while (list_length(rte->eref->colnames) < numCols)
1905	{
1906		rte->eref->colnames = lappend(rte->eref->colnames,
1907									  makeString(pstrdup("?column?")));
1908	}
1909
1910	return parsetree;
1911}
1912
1913/*
1914 * Recursively mark all relations used by a view as FOR [KEY] UPDATE/SHARE.
1915 *
1916 * This may generate an invalid query, eg if some sub-query uses an
1917 * aggregate.  We leave it to the planner to detect that.
1918 *
1919 * NB: this must agree with the parser's transformLockingClause() routine.
1920 * However, we used to have to avoid marking a view's OLD and NEW rels for
1921 * updating, which motivated scanning the jointree to determine which rels
1922 * are used.  Possibly that could now be simplified into just scanning the
1923 * rangetable as the parser does.
1924 */
1925static void
1926markQueryForLocking(Query *qry, Node *jtnode,
1927					LockClauseStrength strength, LockWaitPolicy waitPolicy,
1928					bool pushedDown)
1929{
1930	if (jtnode == NULL)
1931		return;
1932	if (IsA(jtnode, RangeTblRef))
1933	{
1934		int			rti = ((RangeTblRef *) jtnode)->rtindex;
1935		RangeTblEntry *rte = rt_fetch(rti, qry->rtable);
1936
1937		if (rte->rtekind == RTE_RELATION)
1938		{
1939			RTEPermissionInfo *perminfo;
1940
1941			applyLockingClause(qry, rti, strength, waitPolicy, pushedDown);
1942
1943			perminfo = getRTEPermissionInfo(qry->rteperminfos, rte);
1944			perminfo->requiredPerms |= ACL_SELECT_FOR_UPDATE;
1945		}
1946		else if (rte->rtekind == RTE_SUBQUERY)
1947		{
1948			applyLockingClause(qry, rti, strength, waitPolicy, pushedDown);
1949			/* FOR UPDATE/SHARE of subquery is propagated to subquery's rels */
1950			markQueryForLocking(rte->subquery, (Node *) rte->subquery->jointree,
1951								strength, waitPolicy, true);
1952		}
1953		/* other RTE types are unaffected by FOR UPDATE */
1954	}
1955	else if (IsA(jtnode, FromExpr))
1956	{
1957		FromExpr   *f = (FromExpr *) jtnode;
1958		ListCell   *l;
1959
1960		foreach(l, f->fromlist)
1961			markQueryForLocking(qry, lfirst(l), strength, waitPolicy, pushedDown);
1962	}
1963	else if (IsA(jtnode, JoinExpr))
1964	{
1965		JoinExpr   *j = (JoinExpr *) jtnode;
1966
1967		markQueryForLocking(qry, j->larg, strength, waitPolicy, pushedDown);
1968		markQueryForLocking(qry, j->rarg, strength, waitPolicy, pushedDown);
1969	}
1970	else
1971		elog(ERROR, "unrecognized node type: %d",
1972			 (int) nodeTag(jtnode));
1973}
1974
1975
1976/*
1977 * fireRIRonSubLink -
1978 *	Apply fireRIRrules() to each SubLink (subselect in expression) found
1979 *	in the given tree.
1980 *
1981 * NOTE: although this has the form of a walker, we cheat and modify the
1982 * SubLink nodes in-place.  It is caller's responsibility to ensure that
1983 * no unwanted side-effects occur!
1984 *
1985 * This is unlike most of the other routines that recurse into subselects,
1986 * because we must take control at the SubLink node in order to replace
1987 * the SubLink's subselect link with the possibly-rewritten subquery.
1988 */
1989static bool
1990fireRIRonSubLink(Node *node, fireRIRonSubLink_context *context)
1991{
1992	if (node == NULL)
1993		return false;
1994	if (IsA(node, SubLink))
1995	{
1996		SubLink    *sub = (SubLink *) node;
1997
1998		/* Do what we came for */
1999		sub->subselect = (Node *) fireRIRrules((Query *) sub->subselect,
2000											   context->activeRIRs);
2001
2002		/*
2003		 * Remember if any of the sublinks have row security.
2004		 */
2005		context->hasRowSecurity |= ((Query *) sub->subselect)->hasRowSecurity;
2006
2007		/* Fall through to process lefthand args of SubLink */
2008	}
2009
2010	/*
2011	 * Do NOT recurse into Query nodes, because fireRIRrules already processed
2012	 * subselects of subselects for us.
2013	 */
2014	return expression_tree_walker(node, fireRIRonSubLink, context);
2015}
2016
2017
2018/*
2019 * fireRIRrules -
2020 *	Apply all RIR rules on each rangetable entry in the given query
2021 *
2022 * activeRIRs is a list of the OIDs of views we're already processing RIR
2023 * rules for, used to detect/reject recursion.
2024 */
2025static Query *
2026fireRIRrules(Query *parsetree, List *activeRIRs)
2027{
2028	int			origResultRelation = parsetree->resultRelation;
2029	int			rt_index;
2030	ListCell   *lc;
2031
2032	/*
2033	 * Expand SEARCH and CYCLE clauses in CTEs.
2034	 *
2035	 * This is just a convenient place to do this, since we are already
2036	 * looking at each Query.
2037	 */
2038	foreach(lc, parsetree->cteList)
2039	{
2040		CommonTableExpr *cte = lfirst_node(CommonTableExpr, lc);
2041
2042		if (cte->search_clause || cte->cycle_clause)
2043		{
2044			cte = rewriteSearchAndCycle(cte);
2045			lfirst(lc) = cte;
2046		}
2047	}
2048
2049	/*
2050	 * don't try to convert this into a foreach loop, because rtable list can
2051	 * get changed each time through...
2052	 */
2053	rt_index = 0;
2054	while (rt_index < list_length(parsetree->rtable))
2055	{
2056		RangeTblEntry *rte;
2057		Relation	rel;
2058		List	   *locks;
2059		RuleLock   *rules;
2060		RewriteRule *rule;
2061		int			i;
2062
2063		++rt_index;
2064
2065		rte = rt_fetch(rt_index, parsetree->rtable);
2066
2067		/*
2068		 * A subquery RTE can't have associated rules, so there's nothing to
2069		 * do to this level of the query, but we must recurse into the
2070		 * subquery to expand any rule references in it.
2071		 */
2072		if (rte->rtekind == RTE_SUBQUERY)
2073		{
2074			rte->subquery = fireRIRrules(rte->subquery, activeRIRs);
2075
2076			/*
2077			 * While we are here, make sure the query is marked as having row
2078			 * security if any of its subqueries do.
2079			 */
2080			parsetree->hasRowSecurity |= rte->subquery->hasRowSecurity;
2081
2082			continue;
2083		}
2084
2085		/*
2086		 * Joins and other non-relation RTEs can be ignored completely.
2087		 */
2088		if (rte->rtekind != RTE_RELATION)
2089			continue;
2090
2091		/*
2092		 * Always ignore RIR rules for materialized views referenced in
2093		 * queries.  (This does not prevent refreshing MVs, since they aren't
2094		 * referenced in their own query definitions.)
2095		 *
2096		 * Note: in the future we might want to allow MVs to be conditionally
2097		 * expanded as if they were regular views, if they are not scannable.
2098		 * In that case this test would need to be postponed till after we've
2099		 * opened the rel, so that we could check its state.
2100		 */
2101		if (rte->relkind == RELKIND_MATVIEW)
2102			continue;
2103
2104		/*
2105		 * In INSERT ... ON CONFLICT, ignore the EXCLUDED pseudo-relation;
2106		 * even if it points to a view, we needn't expand it, and should not
2107		 * because we want the RTE to remain of RTE_RELATION type.  Otherwise,
2108		 * it would get changed to RTE_SUBQUERY type, which is an
2109		 * untested/unsupported situation.
2110		 */
2111		if (parsetree->onConflict &&
2112			rt_index == parsetree->onConflict->exclRelIndex)
2113			continue;
2114
2115		/*
2116		 * If the table is not referenced in the query, then we ignore it.
2117		 * This prevents infinite expansion loop due to new rtable entries
2118		 * inserted by expansion of a rule. A table is referenced if it is
2119		 * part of the join set (a source table), or is referenced by any Var
2120		 * nodes, or is the result table.
2121		 */
2122		if (rt_index != parsetree->resultRelation &&
2123			!rangeTableEntry_used((Node *) parsetree, rt_index, 0))
2124			continue;
2125
2126		/*
2127		 * Also, if this is a new result relation introduced by
2128		 * ApplyRetrieveRule, we don't want to do anything more with it.
2129		 */
2130		if (rt_index == parsetree->resultRelation &&
2131			rt_index != origResultRelation)
2132			continue;
2133
2134		/*
2135		 * We can use NoLock here since either the parser or
2136		 * AcquireRewriteLocks should have locked the rel already.
2137		 */
2138		rel = table_open(rte->relid, NoLock);
2139
2140		/*
2141		 * Collect the RIR rules that we must apply
2142		 */
2143		rules = rel->rd_rules;
2144		if (rules != NULL)
2145		{
2146			locks = NIL;
2147			for (i = 0; i < rules->numLocks; i++)
2148			{
2149				rule = rules->rules[i];
2150				if (rule->event != CMD_SELECT)
2151					continue;
2152
2153				locks = lappend(locks, rule);
2154			}
2155
2156			/*
2157			 * If we found any, apply them --- but first check for recursion!
2158			 */
2159			if (locks != NIL)
2160			{
2161				ListCell   *l;
2162
2163				if (list_member_oid(activeRIRs, RelationGetRelid(rel)))
2164					ereport(ERROR,
2165							(errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_OBJECT_DEFINITION),
2166							 errmsg("infinite recursion detected in rules for relation \"%s\"",
2167									RelationGetRelationName(rel))));
2168				activeRIRs = lappend_oid(activeRIRs, RelationGetRelid(rel));
2169
2170				foreach(l, locks)
2171				{
2172					rule = lfirst(l);
2173
2174					parsetree = ApplyRetrieveRule(parsetree,
2175												  rule,
2176												  rt_index,
2177												  rel,
2178												  activeRIRs);
2179				}
2180
2181				activeRIRs = list_delete_last(activeRIRs);
2182			}
2183		}
2184
2185		table_close(rel, NoLock);
2186	}
2187
2188	/* Recurse into subqueries in WITH */
2189	foreach(lc, parsetree->cteList)
2190	{
2191		CommonTableExpr *cte = (CommonTableExpr *) lfirst(lc);
2192
2193		cte->ctequery = (Node *)
2194			fireRIRrules((Query *) cte->ctequery, activeRIRs);
2195
2196		/*
2197		 * While we are here, make sure the query is marked as having row
2198		 * security if any of its CTEs do.
2199		 */
2200		parsetree->hasRowSecurity |= ((Query *) cte->ctequery)->hasRowSecurity;
2201	}
2202
2203	/*
2204	 * Recurse into sublink subqueries, too.  But we already did the ones in
2205	 * the rtable and cteList.
2206	 */
2207	if (parsetree->hasSubLinks)
2208	{
2209		fireRIRonSubLink_context context;
2210
2211		context.activeRIRs = activeRIRs;
2212		context.hasRowSecurity = false;
2213
2214		query_tree_walker(parsetree, fireRIRonSubLink, &context,
2215						  QTW_IGNORE_RC_SUBQUERIES);
2216
2217		/*
2218		 * Make sure the query is marked as having row security if any of its
2219		 * sublinks do.
2220		 */
2221		parsetree->hasRowSecurity |= context.hasRowSecurity;
2222	}
2223
2224	/*
2225	 * Apply any row-level security policies.  We do this last because it
2226	 * requires special recursion detection if the new quals have sublink
2227	 * subqueries, and if we did it in the loop above query_tree_walker would
2228	 * then recurse into those quals a second time.
2229	 */
2230	rt_index = 0;
2231	foreach(lc, parsetree->rtable)
2232	{
2233		RangeTblEntry *rte = (RangeTblEntry *) lfirst(lc);
2234		Relation	rel;
2235		List	   *securityQuals;
2236		List	   *withCheckOptions;
2237		bool		hasRowSecurity;
2238		bool		hasSubLinks;
2239
2240		++rt_index;
2241
2242		/* Only normal relations can have RLS policies */
2243		if (rte->rtekind != RTE_RELATION ||
2244			(rte->relkind != RELKIND_RELATION &&
2245			 rte->relkind != RELKIND_PARTITIONED_TABLE))
2246			continue;
2247
2248		rel = table_open(rte->relid, NoLock);
2249
2250		/*
2251		 * Fetch any new security quals that must be applied to this RTE.
2252		 */
2253		get_row_security_policies(parsetree, rte, rt_index,
2254								  &securityQuals, &withCheckOptions,
2255								  &hasRowSecurity, &hasSubLinks);
2256
2257		if (securityQuals != NIL || withCheckOptions != NIL)
2258		{
2259			if (hasSubLinks)
2260			{
2261				acquireLocksOnSubLinks_context context;
2262				fireRIRonSubLink_context fire_context;
2263
2264				/*
2265				 * Recursively process the new quals, checking for infinite
2266				 * recursion.
2267				 */
2268				if (list_member_oid(activeRIRs, RelationGetRelid(rel)))
2269					ereport(ERROR,
2270							(errcode(ERRCODE_INVALID_OBJECT_DEFINITION),
2271							 errmsg("infinite recursion detected in policy for relation \"%s\"",
2272									RelationGetRelationName(rel))));
2273
2274				activeRIRs = lappend_oid(activeRIRs, RelationGetRelid(rel));
2275
2276				/*
2277				 * get_row_security_policies just passed back securityQuals
2278				 * and/or withCheckOptions, and there were SubLinks, make sure
2279				 * we lock any relations which are referenced.
2280				 *
2281				 * These locks would normally be acquired by the parser, but
2282				 * securityQuals and withCheckOptions are added post-parsing.
2283				 */
2284				context.for_execute = true;
2285				(void) acquireLocksOnSubLinks((Node *) securityQuals, &context);
2286				(void) acquireLocksOnSubLinks((Node *) withCheckOptions,
2287											  &context);
2288
2289				/*
